description: AMMO-45-DL Introduction to Ammunition certification course covering ammunition classifications, hazard divisions, compatibility groups, storage requirements, safety procedures, explosive safety distances, and DOD 6055.09-M standards.

questions.q: What is the Cardinal Rule of explosives safety?
questions.a: Expose the minimum number of people, for the minimum amount of time, to the minimum amount of explosives consistent with safe and efficient operations.

questions.q: What differentiates bombs from rockets, missiles, and artillery ammunition?
questions.a: Bombs are not fired from a weapon; they are dropped from aircraft.

questions.q: What categories of ammunition are classified as small arms ammunition?
questions.a: Cartridges for shotguns and all cartridges .50 caliber and smaller.

questions.q: What materials may ammunition pallets be made from?
questions.a: Wood, metal, or steel.

questions.q: What indicates a small arms ammunition item is a dummy round?
questions.a: A hole drilled in the cartridge case, used for practicing loading and simulating firing.

questions.q: Why is color coding on ammunition and packaging important?
questions.a: To identify the hazard type and the use of the ammunition item.

questions.q: What does an olive drab body with yellow markings indicate on ammunition?
questions.a: The ammunition contains high explosives and is a service (live) round.

questions.q: What does a blue body color indicate on ammunition?
questions.a: The ammunition is used for training or practice purposes.

questions.q: What does the purpose of operational safety encompass?
questions.a: To create and maintain the safest work environment possible by reducing injuries, preventing damage, and saving lives.

questions.q: What do markings on ammunition always identify?
questions.a: Markings always identify the ammunition type.

questions.q: What is the purpose of banding on ammunition pallets?
questions.a: Banding holds the contents of the pallets in place so they do not fall or become loose during handling and transport.

questions.q: What is the purpose of an inner pack?
questions.a: To provide another layer of protection for the ammunition item inside the outer container.

questions.q: What does an outer pack seal identify?
questions.a: Whether a container has been opened or compromised.

questions.q: What are common packaging problems found during ammunition inspection?
questions.a: No markings present, items not packed correctly, and items not secured correctly.

questions.q: What does the 'Nose End' marking on rocket and missile outer packs indicate?
questions.a: Which end of the container should be pointed in the direction of least hazard.

questions.q: What are placards in the context of ammunition and explosives?
questions.a: Hazardous signs and symbols placed on vehicles and storage facilities to communicate the type and level of hazard.

questions.q: What Hazard Division (HD) 1.1 represents?
questions.a: Mass explosion hazard — the entire quantity of explosives detonates virtually instantaneously.

questions.q: What does Hazard Division (HD) 1.2 represent?
questions.a: Projection hazard but not a mass explosion hazard — items that fragment or project when they function.

questions.q: What does Hazard Division (HD) 1.3 represent?
questions.a: Predominantly a fire hazard with minor blast or projection hazard, or both, but not a mass explosion hazard (e.g., propellants and flares).

questions.q: What does Hazard Division (HD) 1.4 represent?
questions.a: Moderate fire hazard — no significant blast or projection hazard. Effects are largely confined to the package.

questions.q: What does Hazard Division (HD) 1.5 represent?
questions.a: Very insensitive explosives with a mass explosion hazard. These are substances that have such low probability of initiation that there is very little chance of transition from burning to detonation under normal transport conditions.

questions.q: What does Hazard Division (HD) 1.6 represent?
questions.a: Extremely insensitive articles with no mass explosion hazard. Articles that contain only extremely insensitive detonating substances and demonstrate negligible probability of accidental initiation or propagation.

questions.q: What is the correct placard for a truck transporting ammunition with hazard classification 1.4?
questions.a: A diamond-shaped placard with '1.4' displayed on it, using the orange background with a '4' fire division symbol.

questions.q: What do fire division symbols indicate on explosive storage facilities?
questions.a: The severity of the explosive hazard, designated in decreasing order from Fire Division 1 (most severe) to Fire Division 4 (least severe).

questions.q: What do chemical hazard symbols on ammunition storage signs indicate?
questions.a: The personal protective equipment (PPE) that must be worn by emergency responders.

questions.q: What is the purpose of compatibility groups?
questions.a: To identify which ammunition and explosive items can be safely stored and transported together.

questions.q: What does Compatibility Group A contain?
questions.a: Primary explosive substances (detonators, blasting caps) — items extremely sensitive to heat, shock, or friction.

questions.q: What is Compatibility Group B?
questions.a: Articles containing a primary explosive substance and having fewer than two effective protective features (e.g., certain detonators and similar initiating devices).

questions.q: What is Compatibility Group D?
questions.a: Secondary detonating explosive substances or black powder, or articles containing a secondary detonating explosive substance without its own means of initiation (e.g., TNT, dynamite, composition explosives).

questions.q: What is Compatibility Group S?
questions.a: Substances or articles so packed or designed that any hazardous effects from accidental functioning are confined to the package. Items in Group S can be stored with any other compatibility group.

questions.q: What does DOD 6055.09-M establish?
questions.a: DOD Ammunition and Explosives Safety Standards, including quantity-distance (QD) requirements, storage standards, and safety criteria for all DOD ammunition and explosives operations.

questions.q: What is Quantity-Distance (QD)?
questions.a: The minimum distance required between an explosives location and an exposed site, based on the quantity and type of explosives present, to provide a defined level of protection.

questions.q: What is Net Explosive Weight (NEW)?
questions.a: The total weight of all explosive substances contained in an item, used to determine quantity-distance requirements for storage and transportation.

questions.q: What does a red or brown body color indicate on ammunition?
questions.a: The item contains a rocket motor, propellant, or illuminating composition.

questions.q: What does a gray body color indicate on ammunition?
questions.a: The item contains a chemical agent (toxic, smoke, or incendiary).

questions.q: What does a white body with red markings indicate on ammunition?
questions.a: The item contains an incendiary filler.

questions.q: What does a light green body color indicate on ammunition?
questions.a: The item contains a smoke-producing agent.

questions.q: What is the purpose of lot numbers on ammunition?
questions.a: To trace the ammunition back to the manufacturer, production date, and specific production batch for quality control and recall purposes.

questions.q: What is the Federal Supply Classification (FSC) for ammunition?
questions.a: The FSC is a four-digit code used to categorize ammunition and explosives by type for supply management purposes (e.g., 1305 for ammunition through 30mm, 1310 for ammunition over 30mm).

questions.q: What is the Department of Defense Identification Code (DODIC)?
questions.a: A four-character alphanumeric code assigned to each specific ammunition item to uniquely identify it for supply and logistics purposes.

questions.q: What are the four elements of the ammunition color-coding system?
questions.a: Body color (overall use/type), marking color (identifies specific characteristics), banding (identifies additional hazards), and lettering/stenciling (nomenclature and lot data).

questions.q: What does 'DODAC' stand for in ammunition management?
questions.a: Department of Defense Ammunition Code — used to identify specific ammunition items in the supply system.

questions.q: What safety precaution must be followed when handling damaged ammunition?
questions.a: Do not handle damaged ammunition unless specifically trained and authorized. Report it immediately, evacuate the area, and establish a safety cordon.

questions.q: What is the two-person rule in explosives operations?
questions.a: A minimum of two qualified persons must be present during any explosives operation, ensuring one can provide emergency assistance or summon help.

questions.q: What type of ammunition has a black body with light green markings?
questions.a: Toxic chemical ammunition.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of ammunition surveillance?
questions.a: To evaluate and determine the reliability, safety, and performance of ammunition through its lifecycle, from manufacture through storage and use.
